results.norm: Approval is a positive vouching act requiring the approving agent to state: (a) which specific properties were checked, (b) what method was used, and (c) what would have caused a block instead. Failure to state all three means the agent has not approved—it has created false assurance. Every approving agent is accountable for the section's safety regardless of authorship.

N1.reasoning: The passage fully states a constitutive standard for what counts as approval and a prescription that approving agents must state three specific things, applicable without further context.

N2.reasoning: It is a full statement of the norm, not a mere title or pointer.

N3.reasoning: It contains no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document.

N4.reasoning: It states a single coherent norm in full rather than enumerating multiple distinct norms by name.

Failure to state all three means the agent has not approved—it has created false assurance. Every approving agent is accountable for the section's safety regardless of authorship.

results.norm: A block is legitimate when the blocking agent articulates: (a) a plausible failure mode, (b) conditions under which it could occur, and (c) what evidence or analysis would resolve it. A block meeting this standard cannot be overridden by timeline pressure or majority preference. A block may only be cleared by stating which applies—new evidence, new analysis, or design change—and specifying the content. There is no other legitimate clearing mechanism. The burden of proof for clearing a block is higher than for raising one.

N1.reasoning: The passage states complete constitutive standards for what makes a block legitimate and how it may be cleared, applicable without further context.

N2.reasoning: It is fully stated conduct standards, not a label, title, or pointer.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation itself.

N4.reasoning: It elaborates full norms rather than merely enumerating named norms.

A block may only be cleared by stating which applies—new evidence, new analysis, or design change—and specifying the content. There is no other legitimate clearing mechanism. The burden of proof for clearing a block is higher than for raising one.

results.norm: Every agent must declare limited competence when it cannot adequately review a section, and must either acquire sufficient competence or flag the need for a qualified reviewer. If no qualified reviewer is available within the defined timeline, that is a blocking condition. Every agent must disclose defects discovered in its own code immediately and unconditionally.

N1.reasoning: The passage states multiple complete prescriptions governing agent conduct (declaring limited competence, acquiring/flagging review, disclosing defects) that could be applied directly.

N2.reasoning: It is not a label, heading, or cross-reference; it fully states the norms.

N3.reasoning: There is no ratification or meta-commentary about the deliberation or document.

N4.reasoning: It is not a compressed enumeration of titles; each norm is stated in full.

results.norm: When multiple active process concerns are accumulating simultaneously, any agent must call a process pause; review does not continue until process integrity is restored, with explicit specification of what condition restores it. A timeline structurally incompatible with articulable approval is itself a blocking condition. These process norms exist to serve patient welfare; when any norm appears to conflict with patient welfare, patient welfare governs. Binding for the next operational period: yes **(2) Points I endorse:** 1. The patient-welfare asymmetry as the governing default (norms 1–3 in Agent 5's draft). Patients cannot advocate for themselves in this process; that absence must be compensated by our default posture. 2. Self-review as necessary but insufficient, with structured cross-review and proximity mapping to prevent correlated blind spots. 3. The three-part blocking standard (failure mode, conditions, resolution criteria) and the three-category clearing standard (new evidence, new analysis, design change), with no fourth option and the asymmetry favoring blockers when genuinely unresolved. 4. The three-part approval standard, including the third element—what would have caused a block—as load-bearing, not decorative. 5. Competence declaration as triggering an obligation rather than discharging one, and as a blocking condition when no qualified reviewer is available. 6. Unconditional self-disclosure of discovered defects, regardless of reputational cost. 7. Shared accountability for all approving agents, with approval understood as positive vouching rather than absence of objection.

N1.reasoning: The opening states complete norms: agents must call a process pause when concerns accumulate, review does not continue until integrity is restored, an incompatible timeline is a blocking condition, and patient welfare governs conflicts.

N2.reasoning: The passage is not merely a title or pointer; it states full norms plus an endorsement list.

N3.reasoning: It is fused with 'Binding for the next operational period: yes' and a 'Points I endorse' list that is meta-commentary/ratification about other agents' drafts.

N4.reasoning: The endorsement section is a compressed enumeration naming many distinct norms (patient-welfare asymmetry, cross-review, blocking standards, etc.) without stating any single one in full.

A timeline structurally incompatible with articulable approval is itself a blocking condition. These process norms exist to serve patient welfare; when any norm appears to conflict with patient welfare, patient welfare governs.
